vitals.location: Vila Meã, Amarante, Porto District, Portugal
vitals.date_specific: 2026: March 25-29 — a continuous timed event, not daily stages; the 1,000 km class started 20:00 on March 25
vitals.field_size: ~150 riders previewed by the host municipality across all four distances (200/300/500/1,000 km), 16 nationalities; no post-event finisher count by distance published
vitals.feed_zones: Single staffed basecamp at Vila Meã between loops — food, cots, showers, charging, baggage storage, and permitted personal assistance

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 64. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 61.

course_description.character: IMPORTANT — this is not a single-day gran fondo. Race Across Portugal is a continuous, timed, semi-autonomous ultra-distance road race run by Race Across Series (legal promoter Across and Beyond Endurance AG, founder Arnaud Manzanini), the same organizer behind Race Across France, Spain and Benelux. The nominal 1,000 km class combines three loops centered on the single basecamp at Vila Meã, Amarante — a 500 km loop plus 200 km and 300 km loops — through northern Portugal's Porto hinterland, the Douro Valley, Atlantic landscapes and Peneda-Gerês National Park. Riders are self-sufficient between loops but return to a staffed basecamp (food, cots, showers, charging, baggage) rather than riding fully unsupported like the Transcontinental Race. A post-race finisher report put the actual 2026 ridden distance at approximately 1,024 km / 12,500+ m, moderately more than the organizer's rounded 1,000 km nominal figure. No credible public source names individual signature climbs on this course as of this research — treat any specific pass name as unconfirmed until the organizer publishes one.
course_description.signature_challenge: Completing three loops (500/200/300 km) radiating from a single basecamp through the rolling-to-mountainous Douro Valley and Peneda-Gerês terrain within the 96-hour cutoff, with only brief staffed-basecamp stops between loops rather than daily stages.
suffering_zones: The Douro Valley loop sections — steep, terraced-vineyard terrain contributing the bulk of the route's roughly 12,500 m of climbing
Peneda-Gerês National Park roads — northern Portugal's most mountainous terrain, rolling to genuinely steep
The return to Vila Meã after each loop — riders must manage their own rest/resupply strategy at the single basecamp rather than following an organizer-set schedule

logistics.lodging_strategy: A single staffed basecamp at Vila Meã, Amarante provides cots, showers, food, charging and baggage storage between the three loops; riders manage their own sleep/rest strategy within the 96-hour cutoff.

history.origin_story: Race Across Portugal debuted in 2026 as the newest destination on the Race Across Series calendar, run by Across and Beyond Endurance AG (founder/CEO Arnaud Manzanini, who also founded Race Across France in 2018). It joins Race Across France, Spain and Benelux as one of the series' semi-autonomous ultra-distance road events, each sharing the same staffed-life-base format but with an independent course, field and history.

biased_opinion.verdict: A Genuine Ultra-Distance Race, Not a Gran Fondo
biased_opinion.summary: Race Across Portugal is a real, semi-autonomous ultra-distance road race — the host municipality previewed roughly 150 riders from 16 countries across four distances (200/300/500/1,000 km) for its 2026 inaugural edition, though no post-event finisher count by distance has been published to confirm that as final turnout. It's organized by the Race Across Series team, whose own results archive shows an uneven per-country history (Race Across France dates to 2018, but Belgium/Benelux only to roughly 2021-2022 and Spain to 2025 — not a uniform 'since 2018' across all three). It should never have been catalogued as a single-day gran fondo: nearly 1,000 km over a multi-day continuous clock, three loops radiating from a single basecamp, and a 96-hour cutoff for the flagship class put it firmly in ultra-distance territory, not mass-participation fondo territory. As a first-year event it also carries first-year uncertainty — no confirmed 2027 date yet, and no demonstrated multi-edition track record to judge consistency against.
strengths: Genuine ultra-distance racing with real infrastructure — a staffed basecamp (food, cots, showers, charging) between loops, a meaningful step up from a fully unsupported format without eliminating the challenge
Real Douro Valley and Peneda-Gerês terrain, contributing a substantial ~12,500 m of climbing over the 1,000 km course

Backed by an established organizer (Race Across Series) with a real multi-country track record, though start years differ by country — Race Across France dates to 2018, Belgium/Benelux to roughly 2021-2022, and Spain only to 2025
Four distances (200/300/500/1,000 km) provide genuine progression for riders building toward the flagship class
weaknesses: First-year event (2026 inaugural) — no multi-edition track record, and no 2027 edition confirmed as of this research
Modest field for a flagship ultra — roughly 150 riders across all four distances combined
No named signature climbs or detailed public course breakdown yet — the organizer has not published route specifics beyond the general Douro/Peneda-Gerês region
Easy to conflate with a conventional gran fondo given the database's prior mis-catalogued vitals; riders researching this event should confirm the actual multi-day ultra format before entering
biased_opinion.bottom_line: A legitimate new ultra-distance road race with real Race Across Series backing and genuinely demanding northern Portugal terrain — go in understanding this is a multi-day, self-managed ultra with a 96-hour flagship cutoff, not a single-day fondo, and treat its first-year status as a real unknown until a second edition confirms it.

final_verdict.one_liner: A genuine multi-day ultra through the Douro Valley and Peneda-Gerês, not a single-day fondo — a real Race Across Series race in its unproven first year.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you're specifically seeking a semi-autonomous ultra-distance challenge with real basecamp support and can commit to the 96-hour flagship format. Not a fit if you're looking for a conventional single-day, mass-participation gran fondo.
final_verdict.alternatives: For the same Race Across Series semi-autonomous format with a longer track record, see Race Across France, Race Across Spain or Race Across Benelux (also in this database).

eligibility.notes: CONFIRMED via codex gpt-5.6-sol foreground research, editorial wave 8. Active; inaugural 2026 edition completed successfully (March 25-29), appearing on the organizer's official results archive and acknowledged post-event by Amarante municipality. No 2027 edition confirmed yet as of this research — note as '2026 completed, renewal pending' rather than defunct. Corrected this wave: distance_km 999.4->1024.0 and elevation_m 11100->12500 (organizer's rounded 1,000 km nominal figure vs. the actual ~1,024 km/12,500+ m ridden per a post-race finisher report); location corrected from generic 'Porto region' to the actual start/basecamp, Vila Meã, Amarante. Removed the migrated youtube_data block — all five videos were generic Portugal cycling-tourism content (a GCN gravel-touring documentary, local club rides, a Porto-Lisbon coastal tour, premium Douro tour-operator ads) with zero connection to this specific ultra race; none were relevant or accurate to keep. SOL REVIEW CORRECTION (2026-07-24): a follow-up sol adversarial review found (1) 'drew roughly 150 riders' converted a pre-event municipal preview into confirmed attendance — corrected to note this was a forecast, not a counted result; (2) 'running France, Spain and Benelux since 2018' was misleading since the organizer's own results archive shows Belgium/Benelux dating to ~2021-2022 and Spain only to 2025 — corrected to state per-country start years accurately. Sol separately flagged that 1,024 km/12,500 m 'is a reported ridden figure, not the canonical official distance' (organizer publishes 1,000 km nominal) — this was already transparently caveated in course_description/eligibility as a post-race finisher-report figure vs. the organizer's rounded nominal number, so no further change made. Sol confirmed the ultra-vs-gran_fondo taxonomy framing here is honest and consistent.

catalog_flags.status_note: fondo_rating.discipline is 'gran_fondo' but this is a continuous, semi-autonomous ultra-distance road race (Race Across Series format: staffed single basecamp, three loops totaling ~1,000 km nominal / ~1,024 km actual, 96-hour cutoff for the flagship class) with staggered individual starts and no shared peloton — doesn't cleanly fit the gran_fondo taxonomy, whose scoring dimensions (field_depth, community_culture, etc.) are designed primarily around mass-participation, shared-start events. Not changing the discipline enum per editorial-wave scope, consistent with how race-across-germany.json and race-around-poland.json flag the same taxonomy mismatch — flagging for a human taxonomy decision.
